title: Week 19
summary:
VLCCs
In the Middle East Gulf this week it has been a two-tier market with ‘handicapped ships' being picked off in the low WS 50s. S-Oil took the Genmar Hercules at WS 50.5 basis 280,000 tonnes for Onsan discharge, but this ship has no sire or approvals after change of management. Similarly tonnage ex dry dock has been covered at WS 52.5 by Korean charterers for 270,000 tonnes cargo.

children.title: VLCCs
children.summary: In the Middle East Gulf this week it has been a two-tier market with ‘handicapped ships' being picked off in the low WS 50s. S-Oil took the Genmar Hercules at WS 50.5 basis 280,000 tonnes for Onsan discharge, but this ship has no sire or approvals after change of management.

children.title: Suezmaxes
children.summary: In West Africa, it has been a slower week. Levels were initially holding at WS 80 for Europe discharge, but there is weaker sentiment in the market, borne out by Petrobras who are understood to have covered a short run to Brazil at WS 79 while US Gulf discharge has reportedly been fixed at WS 75.

children.title: Aframaxes
children.summary: The Mediterranean has seen little change with rates holding steady at WS 102.5/105 level while Libya cargoes pay a premium of around 20 points. Delays and uncertainty regarding itineraries for tonnage discharging in Trieste have helped maintain the status quo here.

children.title: Panamaxes
children.summary: It has been a steady week with levels holding at around WS 122.5 having now been done from Continent to US Gulf for 55,000 tonnes, although there is potential for more tonnage to ballast across with Caribbean rates having eased below WS 130 for 50,000 tonnes for up coast discharge.

children.title: Clean
children.summary: A slow week has seen rates gradually ease from a high of WS 157.5 for 37,000 tonnes from Continent to USAC to WS 155 and there is now a report of possibly WS 150 having been agreed here. Rates for 38,000 tonnes from the US Gulf have been steady throughout the week at WS 100, although the main activity here has again tended to see destinations of Central and South America absorbing the lion's share of the enquiry.
